feedback.text: Good day, I would very much welcome the protection of video games from destruction by video game publishers as a topic here. There is currently a huge difference of power between businesses and consumers in ensuring that consumers can keep and use the products they buy. It is now common practice to make video games bought by consumers unusable/unusable for video game publishers by making video games dependent on a server controlled by the publisher. The games are then only playable for as long as the publisher lets this server running. At the same time, there is no plan to allow the consumer to continue to use the product, for example through an offline patch after the publisher no longer supports the product. Moreover, the time for a business to support a video game is not clear to consumers when buying, some games are supported for more than 20 years and others for only about 2 weeks. An example of Letzeres is the Concord video game, which appeared on 23 August 2024 and was no longer playable on 6 September 2024, as the publisher did not want to further support the game. This is not an irreversibility, but a deliberate choice by video game publishers to make these games (often unreasonably) dependent on a central server in order to be able to take back the products already purchased from the consumer. For comparison: Imagine that the manufacturer of a car would upgrade the engine overnight for each car from a particular model because the manufacturer no longer supports this model. Hundreds of video games have already been destroyed by this planned obsolescence and much more are at risk. There is currently a European Citizens’ Initiative entitled ‘Stop Destroying Videogames’, which has collected more than 1.4 million signatures, and the Digital Fairness Act would be the perfect place to place this topic. I hope you will consider this topic in your initiative.
